reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Accurate representation of the wizard flow, grounded in `src/commands/domain-add/index.ts` — the four stages, Parameter Store resumption, root-domain-only restriction, and optional SES step all match the source exactly.
All command examples are in separate fenced bash blocks with no multi-command grouping and no explanatory `#` comments inside the blocks.
`<CliCommandsApiReference>` is used correctly with all args sourced from `commandDefinitions['domain:add']`; no hand-authored flags table.

The DNS ownership section cleanly separates the two scenarios (Route 53 registrar vs external registrar) and accurately describes the name-server-update step, consistent with the source's wizard flow.
FAQ section spans all three required categories: Stacktape-specific, AWS-service-level, and choosing/comparison questions. Answers are concise and stand alone as snippets.
The Warning callout about root-domain-only acceptance is correctly placed and directly grounded in `tldtsParse(domainName).subdomain` check in the source.
problems: The `<FlowDiagram>` component is not in the documented MDX component palette (which includes `<Info>`, `<Warning>`, `<Tip>`, `<CodeBlock>`, `<ApiReference>`, `<ReferenceableParams>`, `<CliCommandsApiReference>`, `<FeatureComparisonTable>`). Using an unknown component risks a rendering failure in production and constitutes use of an unknown MDX component.

The wizard flow diagram omits a critical early-exit path: if `domainStatus.registered` is false (domain not purchased anywhere), the command warns and exits immediately, printing instructions to register via Route 53. This distinct step precedes the DNS ownership check and affects first-time users who haven't purchased a domain yet.

The `<Tip>` at the end of the 'DNS ownership scenarios' section claims the Console 'alerts you when DNS propagation is complete'. The source only shows `tuiManager.hint('Check certificate status in Stacktape Console: https://console.stacktape.com/domains')` — no alert functionality is grounded in the source.

The raw URL `https://console.stacktape.com/domains` appears twice in prose (Tip and final FAQ answer) as unlinked plain text rather than as markdown links, which is inconsistent with how the rest of the page handles URLs.
mandatoryFixes: Replace `<FlowDiagram>` with a supported MDX component or plain prose/numbered list. The component is not in the documented palette and may not render. A bulleted numbered list or `<Info>` callout achieves the same clarity without risking a broken render.

Add the missing early-exit scenario to the wizard description: if the domain is not yet registered (not purchased), the command exits immediately and prints a hint to register via Route 53 or another registrar. This is source-grounded (`if (!domainStatus.registered)` in `src/commands/domain-add/index.ts`) and directly affects a new user's first run.

Remove or narrow the claim 'alerts you when DNS propagation is complete' from the Tip. The source shows only a hint to check the Console URL — no alert mechanism is documented. Replace with: 'The Console shows current certificate validation status at https://console.stacktape.com/domains.'
optionalImprovements: Convert the two plain-text Console URLs in the Tip and FAQ answer to markdown links (`[Stacktape Console](https://console.stacktape.com/domains)`) for consistency with the rest of the page.

The 'What it does' section could note that the wizard exits early at the first incomplete step AND that re-running the command always resumes from the last saved stage — currently this nuance is split between the FlowDiagram description and the FAQ, which makes it harder to find for a first-time reader.

The related commands list includes `secret:create`, which is not naturally related to domain setup. Consider replacing it with a link to the custom domains resource page or the domains-and-certificates docs page referenced in the command's own description.

The FAQ answer for 'What resources can use a custom domain after adding it?' mentions 'private services (via ALB)' — this is extrapolation not grounded in the source, which only mentions 'Web Services, Hosting Buckets, and API Gateways'. Narrow to those three or add a caveat that any ALB-backed resource is eligible.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: The page has a clear CLI-reference shape: command summary, copyable usage, generated argument reference, examples, related commands, and FAQ.
Shell examples follow the one-command-per-block rule and include required `--region` in every invocation.

The root-domain-only constraint, interactive domain prompt, Route 53 hosted zone path, ACM certificate flow, SES DKIM option, and Parameter Store persistence are mostly aligned with the command source.
The FAQ answers real user questions instead of staying purely mechanical.
problems: Several Stacktape-specific claims are not grounded in the provided source files, especially Console behavior, wildcard certificate coverage, exact certificate coverage, and resource compatibility beyond Web Services, Hosting Buckets, and API Gateways.

The TLS section incorrectly implies the command always requests two ACM certificates. Source shows a regional certificate request and a us-east-1 request only when the selected region is not `us-east-1`.

The page claims certificates cover `mydomain.com` and `*.mydomain.com`, and that subdomains are covered by a wildcard certificate. The provided sources only say the domain and subdomains can be used after adding the domain; they do not expose certificate SAN/wildcard details.

The page invents or over-specifies pricing and timing: Route 53 hosted zone `$0.50/month` and DNS propagation `1–48 hours` are not in the provided sources. The source only supports ACM certificates as free, SES verification as free, propagation as `hours to days`, and Route 53 registration starting at `$3/year` for `.click`.

Console UI claims are unsupported because no `console-app` source is provided. The draft says the Console shows real-time certificate validation state, alerts when DNS propagation is complete, and certificate status labels; the source only prints or hints the Console domains URL.

The DNS ownership scenarios are too broad. The source checks `registered`, `ownershipVerified`, and optional hosted zone creation; it does not prove that a domain purchased through Route 53 is always ownership-verified or that external registrars always follow the exact described path.

The FAQ resource list overreaches by naming private services, SSR frontends, ALB, CloudFront, and `customDomains` support where the provided command definition only names Web Services, Hosting Buckets, and API Gateways as examples.
Some H2 sections begin with a code block or component instead of a self-contained paragraph, which weakens extractability for AI/search snippets.
mandatoryFixes: Correct the TLS certificate wording: say the command can request a regional ACM certificate and, when the selected region is not `us-east-1`, a us-east-1 certificate for CloudFront. Do not say it always requests two certificates.

Remove or qualify wildcard/SAN claims. Replace `*.mydomain.com`, `wildcard certificate`, and `CNAME` specifics with source-grounded wording such as `the domain and its subdomains can be used` and `DNS validation record(s)` unless additional source is provided.

Remove unsupported Console workflow claims. Keep only source-grounded wording that the command prints or hints `https://console.stacktape.com/domains` for verifying DNS ownership and checking certificate status.

Remove invented pricing and timing values. Drop Route 53 hosted zone `$0.50/month` and `1–48 hours`; use only sourced pricing/timing: ACM certificates are described as free, SES verification is described as free, Route 53 registration prices start at `$3/year` for `.click`, and propagation can take `hours to days`.

Rewrite DNS ownership scenarios around the actual source states: unregistered domain, DNS managed outside AWS / ownership not verified, optional hosted zone creation, registrar name server update, and rechecking via the Console or rerun. Do not claim Route 53 registration automatically proves ownership unless sourced.

Restrict the list of compatible resources to the sourced command-definition wording: Web Services, Hosting Buckets, and API Gateways, or phrase anything else cautiously only if additional source is available.
Add a short self-contained paragraph immediately after `## Usage`, `## Arguments reference`, and `## Examples` so every H2 has extractable context before code or components.
optionalImprovements: Move `<CliCommandsApiReference command="domain:add" ... />` closer to the usage example to better match the CLI page instruction order, then keep deeper explanation and examples after the generated reference.
Replace the `<FlowDiagram>` with a simple ordered list if the docs component is not part of the established MDX component set for CLI pages.
Add one concise note that `domain:add` is interactive: the domain name is entered at the prompt, while `--region` is the required CLI flag.
Trim repeated FAQ answers that restate earlier sections unless they add a search-oriented angle.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
